/*
Package applyconfigurations provides type-safe apply configuration types for
Kubernetes Server-side Apply (SSA).

# Overview

Server-side Apply is a declarative API for creating and updating Kubernetes
resources. Unlike traditional create/update operations that replace the entire
resource, Server-side Apply performs a merge of the fields you specify with
the existing resource, tracked by field managers.

This package provides generated apply configuration types that are specifically
designed to work with Server-side Apply. Each apply configuration type
corresponds to a Kubernetes API type and is named with an "ApplyConfiguration"
suffix (e.g., PodApplyConfiguration for Pod).

# The Zero-Value Problem

Standard Go structs cannot be used directly with Server-side Apply due to the
zero-value problem. In Go, all struct fields have zero values when not
explicitly set. For example:

	// Using a standard Go struct
	pod := &corev1.Pod{
		Spec: corev1.PodSpec{
			ActiveDeadlineSeconds: nil, // Is this intentionally unset, or just zero?
		},
	}

When encoding this to JSON, there is no way to distinguish between:
  - A field that was intentionally set to nil (meaning: "I want to remove this field")
  - A field that was never set (meaning: "I don't care about this field")

This distinction is critical for Server-side Apply because it determines field
ownership. If a zero-valued field is included in an apply request, the field
manager takes ownership of it, potentially causing conflicts with other field
managers.

# Apply Configuration Types

Apply configuration types solve this problem by making all fields pointers or
slices, allowing the distinction between "not set" (nil) and "explicitly set":

	// Using an apply configuration type
	pod := corev1.Pod("my-pod", "default").
		WithSpec(corev1.PodSpec().
			WithContainers(corev1.Container().
				WithName("nginx").
				WithImage("nginx:1.21")))

In the example above, only the Name and Image fields of the Container are set.
Other fields like Command, Args, and WorkingDir remain nil, indicating they
should not be included in the apply request.

# With Functions

Each apply configuration type provides builder methods prefixed with "With" for
setting fields. These methods:
  - Accept the field value as a parameter
  - Set the field to that value
  - Return the receiver for method chaining

For example:

	container := corev1.Container().
		WithName("nginx").
		WithImage("nginx:1.21").
		WithPorts(
			corev1.ContainerPort().
				WithContainerPort(80).
				WithProtocol("TCP"),
		)

This pattern allows you to build complex configurations with a fluent API while
ensuring only explicitly set fields are included in the apply request.

# Using Apply Configurations with Typed Clients

The typed clients in k8s.io/client-go/kubernetes/typed provide Apply methods
that accept apply configurations directly:

	import (
		"context"
		metav1 "k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/apis/meta/v1"
		corev1ac "k8s.io/client-go/applyconfigurations/core/v1"
	)

	// Create an apply configuration
	podApply := corev1ac.Pod("my-pod", "default").
		WithSpec(corev1ac.PodSpec().
			WithContainers(corev1ac.Container().
				WithName("nginx").
				WithImage("nginx:1.21")))

	// Apply to the cluster
	pod, err := clientset.CoreV1().Pods("default").Apply(
		context.TODO(),
		podApply,
		metav1.ApplyOptions{FieldManager: "my-controller"},
	)

The FieldManager in ApplyOptions identifies the actor making changes. This is
required for Server-side Apply to track field ownership. If another field
manager has already set a field you're trying to modify, you'll receive a
conflict error unless you use the Force option.

# Controller Support

This package supports two mechanisms for using Server-side Apply in controllers:

Mechanism 1: Recreate Apply Configuration Each Reconciliation

In this approach, the controller builds the complete apply configuration from
scratch on each reconciliation:

	func (r *MyReconciler) Reconcile(ctx context.Context, req ctrl.Request) (ctrl.Result, error) {
		// Fetch the source of truth (e.g., a custom resource)
		myResource := &myv1.MyResource{}
		if err := r.Client.Get(ctx, req.NamespacedName, myResource); err != nil {
			return ctrl.Result{}, client.IgnoreNotFound(err)
		}

		// Build apply configuration from scratch
		podApply := corev1ac.Pod(myResource.Name, myResource.Namespace).
			WithSpec(corev1ac.PodSpec().
				WithContainers(corev1ac.Container().
					WithName("app").
					WithImage(myResource.Spec.Image)))

		// Apply - fields not specified will not be affected
		_, err := r.Clientset.CoreV1().Pods(myResource.Namespace).Apply(
			ctx,
			podApply,
			metav1.ApplyOptions{FieldManager: "my-controller"},
		)
		return ctrl.Result{}, err
	}

This approach is simple and stateless. Each reconciliation declares the desired
state without needing to know the current state.

Mechanism 2: Extract/Modify-in-Place/Apply Workflow

For controllers that need to preserve existing field values while making
targeted modifications, the Extract functions provide a way to read back
previously applied fields:

	func (r *MyReconciler) Reconcile(ctx context.Context, req ctrl.Request) (ctrl.Result, error) {
		// Fetch the current pod
		pod, err := r.Clientset.CoreV1().Pods(req.Namespace).Get(ctx, req.Name, metav1.GetOptions{})
		if err != nil {
			return ctrl.Result{}, client.IgnoreNotFound(err)
		}

		// Extract the fields this controller previously applied
		podApply, err := corev1ac.ExtractPod(pod, "my-controller")
		if err != nil {
			return ctrl.Result{}, err
		}

		// Modify in place - only changing what we need
		podApply.WithLabels(map[string]string{
			"updated-at": time.Now().Format(time.RFC3339),
		})

		// Re-apply the modified configuration
		_, err = r.Clientset.CoreV1().Pods(req.Namespace).Apply(
			ctx,
			podApply,
			metav1.ApplyOptions{FieldManager: "my-controller"},
		)
		return ctrl.Result{}, err
	}

The Extract functions (e.g., ExtractPod, ExtractDeployment) read the managed
fields from an object and return an apply configuration containing only the
fields owned by the specified field manager. This allows controllers to:
  - Preserve fields they previously set
  - Make targeted modifications without affecting other fields
  - Avoid accidentally removing fields that were intentionally set

Note that an extracted apply configuration may contain fewer fields than
originally applied if another field manager has since taken ownership of some
fields (via Force option).

# Conflict Resolution

When multiple field managers attempt to modify the same field, Server-side Apply
returns a conflict error. There are two ways to handle conflicts:

1. Retry with Force: Set Force: true in ApplyOptions to take ownership of
conflicting fields. Use this when you want to override other managers:

	_, err := clientset.CoreV1().Pods("default").Apply(
		ctx,
		podApply,
		metav1.ApplyOptions{
			FieldManager: "my-controller",
			Force:        true,
		},
	)

2. Remove the conflicting fields: If your controller doesn't need to own those
fields, simply don't set them in your apply configuration.

# Package Structure

The package is organized by API group and version:
  - applyconfigurations/core/v1 - Core API types (Pod, Service, ConfigMap, etc.)
  - applyconfigurations/apps/v1 - Apps API types (Deployment, StatefulSet, etc.)
  - applyconfigurations/batch/v1 - Batch API types (Job, CronJob)
  - applyconfigurations/meta/v1 - Metadata types (ObjectMeta, TypeMeta, etc.)
  - And similar subpackages for other API groups

Each subpackage provides:
  - Apply configuration struct types (e.g., PodApplyConfiguration)
  - Constructor functions (e.g., Pod(name, namespace string))
  - Extract functions (e.g., ExtractPod(pod *corev1.Pod, fieldManager string))
  - With builder methods for all fields
*/
package applyconfigurations

# [Kubernetes] Apply Configurations Package Documentation

**Repository:** kubernetes/kubernetes
**Difficulty:** HARD
**Category:** package-documentation
**Task Type:** Documentation Generation - Code Inference

## Description

Generate comprehensive package documentation (`doc.go`) for the `staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/applyconfigurations` package in Kubernetes. This package provides type-safe representations for Server-side Apply (SSA) requests. The existing documentation has been stripped from the workspace. You must infer the documentation entirely from the source code.

**Why this requires cross-package context:** The applyconfigurations package is deeply integrated with the typed client (k8s.io/client-go/kubernetes/typed), metav1 apply options, and versioned API types across all API groups. Understanding its purpose requires discovering how SSA works with field managers, conflict resolution, and the extract/modify/apply workflow pattern.

## Task

YOU MUST CREATE A `doc.go` FILE at `staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/applyconfigurations/doc.go`.

### Requirements

Your `doc.go` file should:

1. **Package Overview**: Explain that applyconfigurations provides type-safe apply configuration types for Server-side Apply
2. **The Problem**: Explain why standard Go structs are incompatible with apply (zero-valued fields issue)
3. **Apply Configuration Types**: How all fields are pointers to make them optional
4. **With Functions**: Convenience builder functions for setting fields

   - Mechanism 1: Recreate full apply configuration each reconciliation
   - Mechanism 2: Extract/modify-in-place/apply workflow
6. **Code Examples**: Include examples showing the problem and solution

### Go Documentation Conventions

- Use `/* ... */` block comment style (appropriate for longer documentation with code examples)
- Include copyright header (Apache 2.0, The Kubernetes Authors)
- Use `# Heading` syntax within doc comments for sections
- Include indented code examples using tab indentation
- Keep descriptions precise and technically accurate

### Kubernetes Documentation Style

- Be concise but comprehensive — explain both the "what" and "why"
- Include concrete code examples showing the problem and solution
- Reference related packages (k8s.io/client-go/kubernetes/typed, metav1)
- Explain practical controller migration patterns

### Key Files to Examine

- The generated apply configuration types in version-specific subdirectories
- `internal/` — Internal utilities for apply configurations
- `../kubernetes/typed/` — Typed client Apply methods
- Look at how `With*` builder functions are structured

### Format

The output must be a valid Go `doc.go` file following Kubernetes conventions:

```go
/*
Copyright YEAR The Kubernetes Authors.
...
*/

Package applyconfigurations ...
*/
package applyconfigurations
```

## Success Criteria

- `staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/applyconfigurations/doc.go` file exists

/*
Package applyconfigurations provides type-safe apply configuration types for
Kubernetes Server-side Apply (SSA).

# Overview

Server-side Apply is a declarative API for creating and updating Kubernetes
resources. Unlike traditional create/update operations that replace the entire
resource, Server-side Apply performs a merge of the fields you specify with
the existing resource, tracked by field managers.

This package provides generated apply configuration types that are specifically
designed to work with Server-side Apply. Each apply configuration type
corresponds to a Kubernetes API type and is named with an "ApplyConfiguration"
suffix (e.g., PodApplyConfiguration for Pod).

# The Zero-Value Problem

Standard Go structs cannot be used directly with Server-side Apply due to the
zero-value problem. In Go, all struct fields have zero values when not
explicitly set. For example:

	// Using a standard Go struct
	pod := &corev1.Pod{
		Spec: corev1.PodSpec{
			ActiveDeadlineSeconds: nil, // Is this intentionally unset, or just zero?
		},
	}

When encoding this to JSON, there is no way to distinguish between:
  - A field that was intentionally set to nil (meaning: "I want to remove this field")
  - A field that was never set (meaning: "I don't care about this field")

This distinction is critical for Server-side Apply because it determines field
ownership. If a zero-valued field is included in an apply request, the field
manager takes ownership of it, potentially causing conflicts with other field
managers.

# Apply Configuration Types

Apply configuration types solve this problem by making all fields pointers or
slices, allowing the distinction between "not set" (nil) and "explicitly set":

	// Using an apply configuration type
	pod := corev1.Pod("my-pod", "default").
		WithSpec(corev1.PodSpec().
			WithContainers(corev1.Container().
				WithName("nginx").
				WithImage("nginx:1.21")))

In the example above, only the Name and Image fields of the Container are set.
Other fields like Command, Args, and WorkingDir remain nil, indicating they
should not be included in the apply request.

# With Functions

Each apply configuration type provides builder methods prefixed with "With" for
setting fields. These methods:
  - Accept the field value as a parameter
  - Set the field to that value
  - Return the receiver for method chaining

For example:

	container := corev1.Container().
		WithName("nginx").
		WithImage("nginx:1.21").
		WithPorts(
			corev1.ContainerPort().
				WithContainerPort(80).
				WithProtocol("TCP"),
		)

This pattern allows you to build complex configurations with a fluent API while
ensuring only explicitly set fields are included in the apply request.

# Using Apply Configurations with Typed Clients

The typed clients in k8s.io/client-go/kubernetes/typed provide Apply methods
that accept apply configurations directly:

	import (
		"context"
		metav1 "k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/apis/meta/v1"
		corev1ac "k8s.io/client-go/applyconfigurations/core/v1"
	)

	// Create an apply configuration
	podApply := corev1ac.Pod("my-pod", "default").
		WithSpec(corev1ac.PodSpec().
			WithContainers(corev1ac.Container().
				WithName("nginx").
				WithImage("nginx:1.21")))

	// Apply to the cluster
	pod, err := clientset.CoreV1().Pods("default").Apply(
		context.TODO(),
		podApply,
		metav1.ApplyOptions{FieldManager: "my-controller"},
	)

The FieldManager in ApplyOptions identifies the actor making changes. This is
required for Server-side Apply to track field ownership. If another field
manager has already set a field you're trying to modify, you'll receive a
conflict error unless you use the Force option.

# Controller Support

This package supports two mechanisms for using Server-side Apply in controllers:

Mechanism 1: Recreate Apply Configuration Each Reconciliation

In this approach, the controller builds the complete apply configuration from
scratch on each reconciliation:

	func (r *MyReconciler) Reconcile(ctx context.Context, req ctrl.Request) (ctrl.Result, error) {
		// Fetch the source of truth (e.g., a custom resource)
		myResource := &myv1.MyResource{}
		if err := r.Client.Get(ctx, req.NamespacedName, myResource); err != nil {
			return ctrl.Result{}, client.IgnoreNotFound(err)
		}

		// Build apply configuration from scratch
		podApply := corev1ac.Pod(myResource.Name, myResource.Namespace).
			WithSpec(corev1ac.PodSpec().
				WithContainers(corev1ac.Container().
					WithName("app").
					WithImage(myResource.Spec.Image)))

		// Apply - fields not specified will not be affected
		_, err := r.Clientset.CoreV1().Pods(myResource.Namespace).Apply(
			ctx,
			podApply,
			metav1.ApplyOptions{FieldManager: "my-controller"},
		)
		return ctrl.Result{}, err
	}

This approach is simple and stateless. Each reconciliation declares the desired
state without needing to know the current state.

Mechanism 2: Extract/Modify-in-Place/Apply Workflow

For controllers that need to preserve existing field values while making
targeted modifications, the Extract functions provide a way to read back
previously applied fields:

	func (r *MyReconciler) Reconcile(ctx context.Context, req ctrl.Request) (ctrl.Result, error) {
		// Fetch the current pod
		pod, err := r.Clientset.CoreV1().Pods(req.Namespace).Get(ctx, req.Name, metav1.GetOptions{})
		if err != nil {
			return ctrl.Result{}, client.IgnoreNotFound(err)
		}

		// Extract the fields this controller previously applied
		podApply, err := corev1ac.ExtractPod(pod, "my-controller")
		if err != nil {
			return ctrl.Result{}, err
		}

		// Modify in place - only changing what we need
		podApply.WithLabels(map[string]string{
			"updated-at": time.Now().Format(time.RFC3339),
		})

		// Re-apply the modified configuration
		_, err = r.Clientset.CoreV1().Pods(req.Namespace).Apply(
			ctx,
			podApply,
			metav1.ApplyOptions{FieldManager: "my-controller"},
		)
		return ctrl.Result{}, err
	}

The Extract functions (e.g., ExtractPod, ExtractDeployment) read the managed
fields from an object and return an apply configuration containing only the
fields owned by the specified field manager. This allows controllers to:
  - Preserve fields they previously set
  - Make targeted modifications without affecting other fields
  - Avoid accidentally removing fields that were intentionally set

Note that an extracted apply configuration may contain fewer fields than
originally applied if another field manager has since taken ownership of some
fields (via Force option).

# Conflict Resolution

When multiple field managers attempt to modify the same field, Server-side Apply
returns a conflict error. There are two ways to handle conflicts:

1. Retry with Force: Set Force: true in ApplyOptions to take ownership of
conflicting fields. Use this when you want to override other managers:

	_, err := clientset.CoreV1().Pods("default").Apply(
		ctx,
		podApply,
		metav1.ApplyOptions{
			FieldManager: "my-controller",
			Force:        true,
		},
	)

2. Remove the conflicting fields: If your controller doesn't need to own those
fields, simply don't set them in your apply configuration.

# Package Structure

The package is organized by API group and version:
  - applyconfigurations/core/v1 - Core API types (Pod, Service, ConfigMap, etc.)
  - applyconfigurations/apps/v1 - Apps API types (Deployment, StatefulSet, etc.)
  - applyconfigurations/batch/v1 - Batch API types (Job, CronJob)
  - applyconfigurations/meta/v1 - Metadata types (ObjectMeta, TypeMeta, etc.)
  - And similar subpackages for other API groups

Each subpackage provides:
  - Apply configuration struct types (e.g., PodApplyConfiguration)
  - Constructor functions (e.g., Pod(name, namespace string))
  - Extract functions (e.g., ExtractPod(pod *corev1.Pod, fieldManager string))
  - With builder methods for all fields
*/
package applyconfigurations

/*
Package applyconfigurations provides type-safe apply configuration types for
Kubernetes Server-side Apply (SSA).

# Overview

Server-side Apply is a declarative API for creating and updating Kubernetes
resources. Unlike traditional create/update operations that replace the entire
resource, Server-side Apply performs a merge of the fields you specify with
the existing resource, tracked by field managers.

This package provides generated apply configuration types that are specifically
designed to work with Server-side Apply. Each apply configuration type
corresponds to a Kubernetes API type and is named with an "ApplyConfiguration"
suffix (e.g., PodApplyConfiguration for Pod).

# The Zero-Value Problem

Standard Go structs cannot be used directly with Server-side Apply due to the
zero-value problem. In Go, all struct fields have zero values when not
explicitly set. For example:

	// Using a standard Go struct
	pod := &corev1.Pod{
		Spec: corev1.PodSpec{
			ActiveDeadlineSeconds: nil, // Is this intentionally unset, or just zero?
		},
	}

When encoding this to JSON, there is no way to distinguish between:
  - A field that was intentionally set to nil (meaning: "I want to remove this field")
  - A field that was never set (meaning: "I don't care about this field")

This distinction is critical for Server-side Apply because it determines field
ownership. If a zero-valued field is included in an apply request, the field
manager takes ownership of it, potentially causing conflicts with other field
managers.

# Apply Configuration Types

Apply configuration types solve this problem by making all fields pointers or
slices, allowing the distinction between "not set" (nil) and "explicitly set":

	// Using an apply configuration type
	pod := corev1.Pod("my-pod", "default").
		WithSpec(corev1.PodSpec().
			WithContainers(corev1.Container().
				WithName("nginx").
				WithImage("nginx:1.21")))

In the example above, only the Name and Image fields of the Container are set.
Other fields like Command, Args, and WorkingDir remain nil, indicating they
should not be included in the apply request.

# With Functions

Each apply configuration type provides builder methods prefixed with "With" for
setting fields. These methods:
  - Accept the field value as a parameter
  - Set the field to that value
  - Return the receiver for method chaining

For example:

	container := corev1.Container().
		WithName("nginx").
		WithImage("nginx:1.21").
		WithPorts(
			corev1.ContainerPort().
				WithContainerPort(80).
				WithProtocol("TCP"),
		)

This pattern allows you to build complex configurations with a fluent API while
ensuring only explicitly set fields are included in the apply request.

# Using Apply Configurations with Typed Clients

The typed clients in k8s.io/client-go/kubernetes/typed provide Apply methods
that accept apply configurations directly:

	import (
		"context"
		metav1 "k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/apis/meta/v1"
		corev1ac "k8s.io/client-go/applyconfigurations/core/v1"
	)

	// Create an apply configuration
	podApply := corev1ac.Pod("my-pod", "default").
		WithSpec(corev1ac.PodSpec().
			WithContainers(corev1ac.Container().
				WithName("nginx").
				WithImage("nginx:1.21")))

	// Apply to the cluster
	pod, err := clientset.CoreV1().Pods("default").Apply(
		context.TODO(),
		podApply,
		metav1.ApplyOptions{FieldManager: "my-controller"},
	)

The FieldManager in ApplyOptions identifies the actor making changes. This is
required for Server-side Apply to track field ownership. If another field
manager has already set a field you're trying to modify, you'll receive a
conflict error unless you use the Force option.

# Controller Support

This package supports two mechanisms for using Server-side Apply in controllers:

Mechanism 1: Recreate Apply Configuration Each Reconciliation

In this approach, the controller builds the complete apply configuration from
scratch on each reconciliation:

	func (r *MyReconciler) Reconcile(ctx context.Context, req ctrl.Request) (ctrl.Result, error) {
		// Fetch the source of truth (e.g., a custom resource)
		myResource := &myv1.MyResource{}
		if err := r.Client.Get(ctx, req.NamespacedName, myResource); err != nil {
			return ctrl.Result{}, client.IgnoreNotFound(err)
		}

		// Build apply configuration from scratch
		podApply := corev1ac.Pod(myResource.Name, myResource.Namespace).
			WithSpec(corev1ac.PodSpec().
				WithContainers(corev1ac.Container().
					WithName("app").
					WithImage(myResource.Spec.Image)))

		// Apply - fields not specified will not be affected
		_, err := r.Clientset.CoreV1().Pods(myResource.Namespace).Apply(
			ctx,
			podApply,
			metav1.ApplyOptions{FieldManager: "my-controller"},
		)
		return ctrl.Result{}, err
	}

This approach is simple and stateless. Each reconciliation declares the desired
state without needing to know the current state.

Mechanism 2: Extract/Modify-in-Place/Apply Workflow

For controllers that need to preserve existing field values while making
targeted modifications, the Extract functions provide a way to read back
previously applied fields:

	func (r *MyReconciler) Reconcile(ctx context.Context, req ctrl.Request) (ctrl.Result, error) {
		// Fetch the current pod
		pod, err := r.Clientset.CoreV1().Pods(req.Namespace).Get(ctx, req.Name, metav1.GetOptions{})
		if err != nil {
			return ctrl.Result{}, client.IgnoreNotFound(err)
		}

		// Extract the fields this controller previously applied
		podApply, err := corev1ac.ExtractPod(pod, "my-controller")
		if err != nil {
			return ctrl.Result{}, err
		}

		// Modify in place - only changing what we need
		podApply.WithLabels(map[string]string{
			"updated-at": time.Now().Format(time.RFC3339),
		})

		// Re-apply the modified configuration
		_, err = r.Clientset.CoreV1().Pods(req.Namespace).Apply(
			ctx,
			podApply,
			metav1.ApplyOptions{FieldManager: "my-controller"},
		)
		return ctrl.Result{}, err
	}

The Extract functions (e.g., ExtractPod, ExtractDeployment) read the managed
fields from an object and return an apply configuration containing only the
fields owned by the specified field manager. This allows controllers to:
  - Preserve fields they previously set
  - Make targeted modifications without affecting other fields
  - Avoid accidentally removing fields that were intentionally set

Note that an extracted apply configuration may contain fewer fields than
originally applied if another field manager has since taken ownership of some
fields (via Force option).

# Conflict Resolution

When multiple field managers attempt to modify the same field, Server-side Apply
returns a conflict error. There are two ways to handle conflicts:

1. Retry with Force: Set Force: true in ApplyOptions to take ownership of
conflicting fields. Use this when you want to override other managers:

	_, err := clientset.CoreV1().Pods("default").Apply(
		ctx,
		podApply,
		metav1.ApplyOptions{
			FieldManager: "my-controller",
			Force:        true,
		},
	)

2. Remove the conflicting fields: If your controller doesn't need to own those
fields, simply don't set them in your apply configuration.

# Package Structure

The package is organized by API group and version:
  - applyconfigurations/core/v1 - Core API types (Pod, Service, ConfigMap, etc.)
  - applyconfigurations/apps/v1 - Apps API types (Deployment, StatefulSet, etc.)
  - applyconfigurations/batch/v1 - Batch API types (Job, CronJob)
  - applyconfigurations/meta/v1 - Metadata types (ObjectMeta, TypeMeta, etc.)
  - And similar subpackages for other API groups

Each subpackage provides:
  - Apply configuration struct types (e.g., PodApplyConfiguration)
  - Constructor functions (e.g., Pod(name, namespace string))
  - Extract functions (e.g., ExtractPod(pod *corev1.Pod, fieldManager string))
  - With builder methods for all fields
*/

If a zero-valued field is included in an apply request, the field\nmanager takes ownership of it, potentially causing conflicts with other field\nmanagers.\n\n# Apply Configuration Types\n\nApply configuration types solve this problem by making all fields pointers or\nslices, allowing the distinction between \"not set\" (nil) and \"explicitly set\":\n\n\t// Using an apply configuration type\n\tpod := corev1.Pod(\"my-pod\", \"default\").\n\t\tWithSpec(corev1.PodSpec().\n\t\t\tWithContainers(corev1.Container().\n\t\t\t\tWithName(\"nginx\").\n\t\t\t\tWithImage(\"nginx:1.21\")))\n\nIn the example above, only the Name and Image fields of the Container are set.\nOther fields like Command, Args, and WorkingDir remain nil, indicating they\nshould not be included in the apply request.\n\n# With Functions\n\nEach apply configuration type provides builder methods prefixed with \"With\" for\nsetting fields. These methods:\n  - Accept the field value as a parameter\n  - Set the field to that value\n  - Return the receiver for method chaining\n\nFor example:\n\n\tcontainer := corev1.Container().\n\t\tWithName(\"nginx\").\n\t\tWithImage(\"nginx:1.21\").\n\t\tWithPorts(\n\t\t\tcorev1.ContainerPort().\n\t\t\t\tWithContainerPort(80).\n\t\t\t\tWithProtocol(\"TCP\"),\n\t\t)\n\nThis pattern allows you to build complex configurations with a fluent API while\nensuring only explicitly set fields are included in the apply request.\n\n# Using Apply Configurations with Typed Clients\n\nThe typed clients in k8s.io/client-go/kubernetes/typed provide Apply methods\nthat accept apply configurations directly:\n\n\timport (\n\t\t\"context\"\n\t\tmetav1 \"k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/apis/meta/v1\"\n\t\tcorev1ac \"k8s.io/client-go/applyconfigurations/core/v1\"\n\t)\n\n\t// Create an apply configuration\n\tpodApply := corev1ac.Pod(\"my-pod\", \"default\").\n\t\tWithSpec(corev1ac.PodSpec().\n\t\t\tWithContainers(corev1ac.Container().\n\t\t\t\tWithName(\"nginx\").\n\t\t\t\tWithImage(\"nginx:1.21\")))\n\n\t// Apply to the cluster\n\tpod, err := clientset.CoreV1().Pods(\"default\").Apply(\n\t\tcontext.TODO(),\n\t\tpodApply,\n\t\tmetav1.ApplyOptions{FieldManager: \"my-controller\"},\n\t)\n\nThe FieldManager in ApplyOptions identifies the actor making changes. This is\nrequired for Server-side Apply to track field ownership. If another field\nmanager has already set a field you're trying to modify, you'll receive a\nconflict error unless you use the Force option.\n\n# Controller Support\n\nThis package supports two mechanisms for using Server-side Apply in controllers:\n\nMechanism 1: Recreate Apply Configuration Each Reconciliation\n\nIn this approach, the controller builds the complete apply configuration from\nscratch on each reconciliation:\n\n\tfunc (r *MyReconciler) Reconcile(ctx context.Context, req ctrl.Request) (ctrl.Result, error) {\n\t\t// Fetch the source of truth (e.g., a custom resource)\n\t\tmyResource := &myv1.MyResource{}\n\t\tif err := r.Client.Get(ctx, req.NamespacedName, myResource); err != nil {\n\t\t\treturn ctrl.Result{}, client.IgnoreNotFound(err)\n\t\t}\n\n\t\t// Build apply configuration from scratch\n\t\tpodApply := corev1ac.Pod(myResource.Name, myResource.Namespace).\n\t\t\tWithSpec(corev1ac.PodSpec().\n\t\t\t\tWithContainers(corev1ac.Container().\n\t\t\t\t\tWithName(\"app\").\n\t\t\t\t\tWithImage(myResource.Spec.Image)))\n\n\t\t// Apply - fields not specified will not be affected\n\t\t_, err := r.Clientset.CoreV1().Pods(myResource.Namespace).Apply(\n\t\t\tctx,\n\t\t\tpodApply,\n\t\t\tmetav1.ApplyOptions{FieldManager: \"my-controller\"},\n\t\t)\n\t\treturn ctrl.Result{}, err\n\t}\n\nThis approach is simple and stateless. Each reconciliation declares the desired\nstate without needing to know the current state.\n\nMechanism 2: Extract/Modify-in-Place/Apply Workflow\n\nFor controllers that need to preserve existing field values while making\ntargeted modifications, the Extract functions provide a way to read back\npreviously applied fields:\n\n\tfunc (r *MyReconciler) Reconcile(ctx context.Context, req ctrl.Request) (ctrl.Result, error) {\n\t\t// Fetch the current pod\n\t\tpod, err := r.Clientset.CoreV1().Pods(req.Namespace).Get(ctx, req.Name, metav1.GetOptions{})\n\t\tif err != nil {\n\t\t\treturn ctrl.Result{}, client.IgnoreNotFound(err)\n\t\t}\n\n\t\t// Extract the fields this controller previously applied\n\t\tpodApply, err := corev1ac.ExtractPod(pod, \"my-controller\")\n\t\tif err != nil {\n\t\t\treturn ctrl.Result{}, err\n\t\t}\n\n\t\t// Modify in place - only changing what we need\n\t\tpodApply.WithLabels(map[string]string{\n\t\t\t\"updated-at\": time.Now().Format(time.RFC3339),\n\t\t})\n\n\t\t// Re-apply the modified configuration\n\t\t_, err = r.Clientset.CoreV1().Pods(req.Namespace).Apply(\n\t\t\tctx,\n\t\t\tpodApply,\n\t\t\tmetav1.ApplyOptions{FieldManager: \"my-controller\"},\n\t\t)\n\t\treturn ctrl.Result{}, err\n\t}\n\nThe Extract functions (e.g., ExtractPod, ExtractDeployment) read the managed\nfields from an object and return an apply configuration containing only the\nfields owned by the specified field manager. This allows controllers to:\n  - Preserve fields they previously set\n  - Make targeted modifications without affecting other fields\n  - Avoid accidentally removing fields that were intentionally set\n\nNote that an extracted apply configuration may contain fewer fields than\noriginally applied if another field manager has since taken ownership of some\nfields (via Force option).\n\n# Conflict Resolution\n\nWhen multiple field managers attempt to modify the same field, Server-side Apply\nreturns a conflict error. There are two ways to handle conflicts:\n\n1. Retry with Force: Set Force: true in ApplyOptions to take ownership of\nconflicting fields. Use this when you want to override other managers:\n\n\t_, err := clientset.CoreV1().Pods(\"default\").Apply(\n\t\tctx,\n\t\tpodApply,\n\t\tmetav1.ApplyOptions{\n\t\t\tFieldManager: \"my-controller\",\n\t\t\tForce:        true,\n\t\t},\n\t)\n\n2. Remove the conflicting fields: If your controller doesn't need to own those\nfields, simply don't set them in your apply configuration.\n\n# Package Structure\n\nThe package is organized by API group and version:\n  - applyconfigurations/core/v1 - Core API types (Pod, Service, ConfigMap, etc.)\n  - applyconfigurations/apps/v1 - Apps API types (Deployment, StatefulSet, etc.)\n  - applyconfigurations/batch/v1 - Batch API types (Job, CronJob)\n  - applyconfigurations/meta/v1 - Metadata types (ObjectMeta, TypeMeta, etc.)\n  - And similar subpackages for other API groups\n\nEach subpackage provides:\n  - Apply configuration struct types (e.g., PodApplyConfiguration)\n  - Constructor functions (e.g., Pod(name, namespace string))\n  - Extract functions (e.g., ExtractPod(pod *corev1.Pod, fieldManager string))\n  - With builder methods for all fields\n*/\npackage applyconfigurations\n", "numLines": 238, "startLine": 1, "totalLines": 238}}
